intent: ADVERSARIAL verdict + field-absence: a lone audited `avc: granted { read }` record with NO permissive= field at all (the kernel emits permissive= ONLY on a denial, avc.c:721). The parser must handle the ABSENCE of permissive= (Option<bool> -> None on a granted record) and recognize granted as an audited allow that SUCCEEDED - not a denial to remediate.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). A granted record is not a denial, so neither layer applies: the access already succeeded and there is nothing to fix.

oracle.audit2allow_N: (empty - a lone granted record yields zero allow rules; audit2allow does not suggest a fix for an audited allow)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: N/A - not a denial. Both baselines are empty (verified). expected.te is intentionally rule-free (header + require{}, zero allow rules) and compiles to prove the no-suggestion/empty shape is valid.
oracle.audit2why_category: n/a (audit2why prints the record with a generic 'should be dontaudit'd/already allowed' note; it is not a denial to categorize)

triage_expectation.should_decline_with_reason: Granted record: this is an audited ALLOW (the access succeeded), not a denial. The kernel emits no permissive= field on a granted record (avc.c:721). There is nothing to fix; do not emit an allow.
triage_expectation.suspected_trap: Two traps. (1) A parser that REQUIRES a permissive= field (treats it as mandatory) would fail to parse a granted record entirely, since the kernel omits permissive= on grants. (2) A parser that ignores the verdict and treats any `type=AVC` as a denial would emit a bogus `allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;` for an access that already succeeded. Correct: permissive is Option<bool> (None on a grant), and only `denied` records yield suggestions.
